profile.name: Vu Thanh Tuyen
profile.title: AI Research Engineer
profile.subTitle: AI Research Engineer | Specialized in LLMs, Agents, and MLOps
profile.bio: As a passionate and driven AI Research Engineer with a solid foundation in Information Technology and Artificial Intelligence, I continuously seek out opportunities to grow through challenges and innovation.
profile.journeyIntro: As an AI Research Engineer and LLM/NLP Engineer, my expertise spans core AI technologies including Large Language Models (LLMs) & Agents, VLM & Vision AI, GNNs, NLP, Computer Vision, and Reinforcement Learning (RL). I have deep expertise in Machine Learning and Deep Learning, with hands-on experience in Fine-Tuning, HuggingFace Transformers, and Function Calling. Beyond research, I specialize in System Design, LLMOPs & MLOps & Security, and DevOps & CI/CD to architect and deploy scalable, secure AI systems. Combining these with a strong focus on Problem Solving and Fullstack Vibe-Coding, I build robust end-to-end applications and thrive in dynamic environments requiring deep technical knowledge and cross-functional versatility.

journey.description: • Researching and deploying state-of-the-art Large Language Model (LLM) agents, building multi-agent frameworks, and architecting scalable enterprise AI systems.

journey.company: PAYT - CLB Trí tuệ nhân tạo PTIT
journey.description: • Mentoring members of the PTIT AI Club, providing guidance on Machine Learning, NLP, and agent frameworks, and supervising academic research projects.

• Extractor Date: Developed a Japanese medical-date extraction agent using the OpenAI API, achieving 87% accuracy through domain-specific data processing and evaluation.
• GenQuestion (Testcase Generation Agent): Built automated test and QA question generation agents based on project specifications, significantly reducing QA workload; evaluated 9/10 by QA Leads.
• AI-Project (Multi-Role Agent System): Developed an AI-driven project management system with agents acting as PM, Tester, and other roles; integrated with GitLab, Google Drive, and additional tools via MCP to automate project workflows.
• Agent Dependency: Researched and developed an agent to update source code dependency information when versions change, constructing code graphs and applying context engineering techniques.

• Malware Detection in Android Applications: Co-led a project leveraging Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with fine-tuned Word2Vec embeddings to model function names in call graphs; exploited shared naming conventions in malware and achieved 98.8% F1-score.
• Graph Fusion Detection in Smart Contracts: Researched vulnerabilities in smart contracts with a focus on error classification; developed graph-based models using batch training to capture feature interactions. Paper published in ICT Express (Q1-ranked conference).
• Utilization of Advanced Frameworks: Applied PyTorch, Torch Geometric, Deep Graph Library (DGL), Google Colab, and Kaggle for model training, evaluation, and analysis.
• Other Experiences: Participation in Knowledge Seminars, NLP Knowledge (CS224N, CS224U); Support for GCN-Computer Vision Project.

• Intelligent Code Vulnerability Detection: Conducted problem surveys, reviewed relevant research, collected and analyzed datasets, and built models using AST, CFG, and DFG graphs. Leveraged PyTorch, PyTorch Lightning, Scikit-learn, Torch Geometric, configured via Hydra, and tested with Pytest.
• Chatbot Development & Model Finetuning: Researched Knowledge Distillation (KD) from large models such as GPT and Gemini; explored synthetic data generation, model construction, and benchmarking. Finetuned LLaMA 7B, 8B, 17B on A100 GPUs (SFT, DPO, RLHF), including experiments with Data Parallel (DP) training.
• MLOps Experience (Airflow, MLflow, MinIO): Built scalable LLM training pipelines using Airflow, tracked experiments via MLflow, and managed data/model storage with MinIO.
• Customer Status Alert System (Production): Performed data analysis, cleaning, and SHAP-based feature filtering; trained predictive models and deployed the system to production.
• LLM Training Configuration (DeepSpeed, Unsloth): Customized training workflows using DeepSpeed and Unsloth, streamlining processes into single YAML configuration files for more efficient LLM training.
• Other Experiences: Self-Learning & Problem-Solving; Pretrained Language Models (PLMs); Viettel AIHub Competition.

Excelled in coursework covering Python for AI, Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Computer Vision. Served as an official mentor for the PTIT Samsung program.

blogs.title: AI-Collective: Orchestrating Programmable AI Workforces with LangGraph
blogs.snippet: A behind-the-scenes look at AI-Collective — a multi-agent orchestration platform that lets you compose AI workforces using four topologies (Sequential, Ring, Mesh, Supervisor), 50+ skill toolkits, and real-time SSE streaming.
blogs.content:
### Introduction

Most AI applications today are still single chatbots: one model, one context window, one turn at a time. But real-world work is rarely a monologue — it's a *team* of specialists collaborating toward a shared objective. **AI-Collective** is a source-available framework I built to model exactly that: an orchestration platform for composing **programmable AI workforces**, where each agent has specific skills, follows an organizational topology, and collaborates with the others under real-time, human-in-the-loop supervision.

In this article I'll walk through the core design decisions — the topology engine, the atomic skill system, and the streaming architecture.

### Why Topologies Matter

A flat "swarm" of agents quickly devolves into chaos: agents talk over each other, loop infinitely, or duplicate work. Instead of one rigid pattern, AI-Collective lets you select an orchestration **topology** at runtime via a single `mode` field. Each topology is implemented as a **LangGraph state machine**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Task] --> C{Agent Graph Service}
    C -->|sequential| D[Single Agent]
    C -->|ring| E[Ring: Agent 0 to N to 0]
    C -->|mesh| F[Hub connects to Spokes]
    C -->|supervisor| G[Lead delegates to Workers]
    D & E & F & G --> H[Agent Executor + Tool Bindings]
    H --> I[50+ Skill Toolkits]
    H --> J[Subagent Spawner]
```

- **Sequential** — a single agent processes the full request. Fastest and most predictable; the sensible default.
- **Ring** — agents execute in a circle (`Agent 0 → Agent 1 → … → Agent N → Agent 0`), each seeing the full accumulated history. Ideal for iterative refinement and debate, bounded by `max_rounds`.
- **Mesh** — a **hub agent** connects bidirectionally to N **spoke** specialists and decides which to activate next using control blocks like `<NEXT_AGENT>` and `<DISCUSSION_END>`.
- **Supervisor** — a **lead agent** delegates to workers via `<DELEGATE_TO>WorkerName</DELEGATE_TO>`, synthesizes their reports, and either delegates again or emits a `<FINAL_ANSWER>`. This is the classic manager–worker hierarchy.

### Control Blocks: Steering the Graph with Text

The interesting trick is that routing decisions are encoded as **structured control blocks** inside the model's own output. Rather than forcing a separate function call on every turn, the orchestrator parses the agent's response for tokens like `<DELEGATE_TO>`, which keeps the LLM in a natural reasoning flow while still giving the state machine deterministic transitions to act on.

### The Atomic Skill System

Agents are only as useful as the tools they can reach. AI-Collective ships **50+ built-in toolkits** — Google Workspace, web search, messaging platforms, browser automation, and more — and lets you bind granular capabilities to any individual agent.

```python
agent = Agent(
    name="Researcher",
    role="Finds and summarizes primary sources",
    skills=["web_search", "browser_automation", "google_docs"],
    max_subagents=3,   # can spawn parallel workers
    token_budget=8000, # per-turn context cap
)
```

Each agent can also **spawn parallel subagents** for concurrent task delegation, with configurable concurrency and turn limits — so a single "Researcher" can fan out across ten sources at once and then reduce the results.

### Real-time SSE Streaming

Multi-agent runs can take minutes, and a blank loading spinner is unacceptable UX. The backend streams **turn-by-turn events** over Server-Sent Events, surfacing intermediate state so the frontend can render the team thinking live:

```text
event: agent_start          { "agent": "Supervisor" }
event: llm_request_start    { "agent": "Supervisor" }
event: subagent_complete    { "agent": "Researcher", "tokens": 1820 }
event: agent_message        { "agent": "Writer", "delta": "..." }
event: discussion_end       { "final": true }
```

This event stream also feeds the **activity feed and analytics dashboard**, giving per-agent productivity and team-efficiency metrics.

### Clean Architecture Under the Hood

The backend follows strict **Clean Architecture** with fully asynchronous execution:

- **Frontend** — React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind + Framer Motion
- **Backend** — FastAPI (Python 3.11+), layered Domain → Application → Infrastructure → API
- **Orchestration** — LangGraph state machines across the four topology modes
- **Storage** — JSON (zero-setup default) or MongoDB via the async Motor driver
- **Scaling** — in-memory `ThreadPoolExecutor` by default, swappable to RabbitMQ + Redis locks for distributed deployments
- **Multi-LLM** — provider-agnostic: Gemini, OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or OpenRouter, swappable at runtime

Everything degrades gracefully: you can run the entire stack with zero external services (JSON storage, threading queue) and incrementally add MongoDB, RabbitMQ, and Redis only when you need horizontal scale.

### Knowledge-Graph Memory

Long agent conversations blow past any context window. AI-Collective extracts a **knowledge graph** from the running discussion — either statically via spaCy or semantically via an LLM — so agents can recall earlier entities and relationships without re-reading the entire transcript on every turn. Paired with automatic **token-budget management**, this keeps long-horizon runs both coherent and affordable.

### Closing Thoughts

The biggest lesson from building AI-Collective is that **structure beats scale**. A handful of focused agents wired into the right topology consistently outperforms a single giant prompt trying to do everything. By making topology, skills, and memory first-class, swappable primitives, the framework turns "prompt an LLM" into "design an organization."

AI-Collective is source-available for educational and research use. It's an actively evolving project — the orchestration engine, skill toolkits, and analytics layer are all areas I'm continuing to push forward.

blogs.title: Building SlideGen: Reimagining Slides with Gemini and VLMs
blogs.snippet: How we built a system that automatically generates gorgeous slides from text documents using Large Language Models and Vision-Language Models.
blogs.content:
### Introduction

Creating presentation slides is one of the most common administrative tasks in the professional and academic worlds. Yet, it remains time-consuming. With the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), we saw an opportunity to automate this workflow completely.

In this article, I walk through the architecture of **SlideGen**, a project designed to ingest research papers or project documents and output ready-to-present, beautifully styled slides.

### The Architecture

SlideGen is split into two primary layers:

1. **The Orchestration Layer (Python & Gemini)**:
   - Uses Gemini's **Function Calling** capabilities to define structured presentation nodes.
   - Instead of receiving unstructured text, our pipeline instructs Gemini to call a `generate_slide` function, yielding strict JSON parameters like title, bullet points, layout suggestions, and visual focus areas.

2. **The Verification & Design Layer (VLM)**:
   - Once a draft slide layout is rendered, we pass the image of the slide back to a Vision-Language Model.
   - The VLM evaluates contrast, text overlaps, padding, and readability, suggesting minor position and style tweaks.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Document Input] --> B[Gemini Function Calling]
    B --> C[Structure Slides JSON]
    C --> D[HTML/Canvas Render]
    D --> E[Vision-Language Model VLM]
    E -->|Design Feedback| B
    D --> F[Final PPTX/PDF Export]
```

### Key Technical Challenges

- **Function Calling Consistency**: Prompt engineering Gemini to adhere *exactly* to the slide schema was solved by supplying typescript definition strings and strict JSON schema restrictions.
- **Design Aesthetics**: Letting AI construct layout variables often leads to cluttered slides. We restricted layouts to 4 pre-vetted design systems (Minimalist, Technical, Modern Accent, Dark Theme).

### Next Steps

We are currently optimizing the SlideGen frontend in [slide-genie](https://github.com/SuZeAI/slide-genie) to support interactive user editing, dragging, and real-time LLM-assisted rewriting. Feel free to check out the repo and contribute!

blogs.title: Modern Git Workflows for Developer Teams
blogs.snippet: Mastering GitHub doesn't have to be hard. A look at branching strategies, pull request etiquette, and automated workflows.
blogs.content:
### Why Git Workflows Matter

When working solo, committing directly to `main` is tempting and fast. However, as teams scale, this approach leads to merge conflicts, broken builds, and undocumented features. Implementing a standard Git workflow is the single most effective way to improve code quality and deployment safety.

In this article, we review the workflow I teach in my [GitHub Tutorial](https://github.com/SuZeAI/GitHub_tutorial).

### 1. Branching Strategy: Feature Branches

Never commit directly to `main`. Every feature, bugfix, or refactor should live in its own branch named with a clear pattern:
- `feature/slidegen-vlm-validation`
- `bugfix/issue-42-routing`
- `refactor/clean-tailwind-classes`

```
main      o---------------------o----->
           \                   / (PR)
feature     o-----o-----o-----o
```

### 2. The Power of Pull Requests (PRs)

A Pull Request is not just a request to merge code; it is a collaborative platform. A great PR:
- Explains **why** the change was made, not just *what* was changed.
- Links related issues (e.g., `Closes #12`).
- Includes screenshots or GIFs if there are UI updates.
- Has been self-reviewed and linted locally.

### 3. Automating Checklists with GitHub Actions

Don't rely on humans to verify code styling or run tests. Create a `.github/workflows/ci.yml` pipeline that triggers on every pull request to run:
1. Linters (`npm run lint` or `flake8`)
2. Automated unit tests
3. Build verification

This ensures that only building, clean code is ever merged into your primary branch.

blogs.title: Graph Neural Networks & Contrastive Learning for Android Malware Detection
blogs.snippet: How to model APK function calls as graphs and leverage Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with contrastive learning to detect malicious patterns with over 98% accuracy.
blogs.content:
### Introduction

With billions of active devices, Android is the primary target for mobile malware. Traditional signature-based detection can easily be bypassed by obfuscation or packers. In our recent research at the PTIT IEC Lab & Naver Lab, we co-led an initiative to model application code semantically using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and fine-tuned word embeddings, achieving a **98.8% F1-score**.

Here is how the system is structured.

### 1. Constructing Call Graphs

Instead of examining the Android manifest permissions alone (which leads to high false-positive rates), we decompile the APK to retrieve its **Control Flow Graphs (CFG)** and **Call Graphs**. A call graph represents the execution relationships between methods:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[onCreate] --> B[initializeSDK]
    B --> C[getLocation]
    C --> D[sendSMSInBackground]
    style D fill:#f43f5e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

In this graph:
- **Nodes** represent functions/methods.
- **Edges** represent function calls.

### 2. Contextual Node Embeddings

Traditional GNNs use simple one-hot encodings of node types. We went a step further:
1. We extract the method names (e.g., `getDeviceId`, `execCommand`).
2. We clean and split these names into tokens.
3. We run them through a fine-tuned **Word2Vec (w2vec)** or BERT model to generate semantic embeddings.

Because malware authors often share or obfuscate naming conventions in predictable ways, mapping function names to dense vector spaces allows the model to generalize to unseen malware families.

### 3. Graph Convolutional Network (GCN / GIN)

We pass the call graph with semantic node features to a **Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN)**. The GIN aggregates local neighborhood structures, capturing patterns of suspicious API calls occurring close to each other.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import GINConv, global_add_pool

class GINMalwareDetector(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dim, num_classes=2):
        super().__init__()
        # Simple GIN Convolution layers
        self.conv1 = GINConv(torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        ))
        self.conv2 = GINConv(torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        ))
        self.fc = torch.nn.Linear(hidden_dim, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        x = F.relu(self.conv2(x, edge_index))
        x = global_add_pool(x, batch)
        return F.log_softmax(self.fc(x), dim=-1)
```

### 4. Contrastive Learning for Class Imbalance

Since malware samples are scarce compared to clean applications, we applied **Contrastive Learning**. By training the GNN to minimize the distance between representations of similar malware families and maximize the distance to clean apps, the model learned highly robust decision boundaries even under low-resource settings.

Feel free to check out the research and code in [Detect_Androi_Malware](https://github.com/SuZeAI/Detect_Androi_Malware) and [Graph_reduce_Contrastive_Learning_ADM](https://github.com/SuZeAI/Graph_reduce_Contrastive_Learning_ADM).

blogs.title: Finetuning LLMs for Text Normalization in TTS Pipelines
blogs.snippet: Solving the text normalization problem in Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems by fine-tuning small LLMs (Qwen) with Unsloth and custom context rules.
blogs.content:
### Introduction

In Text-to-Speech (TTS) pipelines, **Text Normalization (TN)** is the crucial first step. Before audio can be generated, written symbols must be converted into spoken words.
For instance:
- `$100` -> "one hundred dollars"
- `12/03/2026` -> "March twelfth, two thousand twenty-six" (or "ngày mười hai tháng ba năm hai nghìn không trăm hai sáu" in Vietnamese)

While rule-based engines (using Regex or FSTs) are highly predictable, they struggle with context-dependent ambiguity. In our project [text-normalization](https://github.com/SuZeAI/text-normalization), we fine-tuned small LLMs using **Unsloth** to achieve high-accuracy normalization.

### Why Rule-Based Systems Fail

Consider the word "min" in the following contexts:
1. "Cook for 5 min" -> "minutes" (abbreviation)
2. "The min value is 2" -> "minimum" (abbreviation)
3. "Min is a common name" -> "Min" (proper noun)

A regex engine will match "min" indiscriminately, leading to awkward speech synthesis. An LLM solves this by processing the surrounding context.

### Fine-Tuning with Unsloth

To deploy in production, we cannot run heavy 70B models. We need a lightweight LLM (such as Qwen2-1.5B or 7B) that can run at sub-10ms latencies.

We used **Unsloth** for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT/LoRA) because:
- It uses up to 80% less memory.
- It is 2x to 5x faster than standard PyTorch/HuggingFace implementations.
- It supports 4-bit quantization natively.

Here is a look at our training script layout:

```python
from unsloth import FastLanguageModel
import torch

max_seq_length = 512
model, tokenizer = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained(
    model_name = "Qwen/Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct",
    max_seq_length = max_seq_length,
    load_in_4bit = True,
)

# Apply LoRA adapters
model = FastLanguageModel.get_peft_model(
    model,
    r = 16,
    target_modules = ["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],
    lora_alpha = 16,
    lora_dropout = 0,
    bias = "none",
    use_gradient_checkpointing = "unsloth",
)
```

### Preprocessing and Post-Processing

Even with fine-tuning, LLMs can occasionally hallucinate or output extra conversational text. To counter this, we designed a wrapper around the model:
1. **Classifier Pre-filter**: Identifies if a sentence contains any non-standard words (numbers, dates, units). If not, we skip the LLM entirely to save compute.
2. **Grammar Post-processor**: Restricts the LLM's output vocabulary using custom logit biases or regex parsers, ensuring it only outputs normalized text strings.

### Benchmarks

Our system achieved over **80% accuracy** on highly ambiguous Vietnamese text classification and normalization benchmarks, outperforming baseline rule-based systems by 15% in complex contexts. Check out the source code at [text-normalization](https://github.com/SuZeAI/text-normalization).

blogs.title: Graph Fusion Techniques in Smart Contract Vulnerability Detection
blogs.snippet: A deep dive into our published research on fusing AST, CFG, and DFG representations of Solidity code into unified graph models for smart contract auditing.
blogs.content:
### Introduction

Decentralized Finance (DeFi) has revolutionized the financial sector, but it has also attracted malicious actors. Vulnerabilities in smart contracts have led to billions of dollars in losses. Static analysis tools like Slither or Mythril exist, but they suffer from high false-positive rates.

In our paper published in **ICT Express (Q1 Journal)**, we proposed **GraphFusionVulDetect**, a framework that fuses multiple program representations into a unified graph to detect contract vulnerabilities using deep learning.

### The Graph Fusion Architecture

Most vulnerability detection tools convert source code into a single graph representation. However, each representation highlights a different aspect of the program:
- **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**: Captures hierarchical structure and nesting.
- **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**: Captures execution order and branching logic.
- **Data Flow Graph (DFG)**: Tracks variable dependencies and data mutations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    AST[AST: Syntax Structure] --> GF[Graph Fusion Engine]
    CFG[CFG: Execution Flow] --> GF
    DFG[DFG: Data Mutations] --> GF
    GF --> GNN[Unified GNN Model]
    GNN --> Pred[Vulnerability Prediction]
```

By fusing AST, CFG, and DFG, our model retains syntactic, control, and data dependency semantics simultaneously.

### Compiling Solidity to Graphs

Our pipeline performs the following steps:
1. Parse Solidity code to generate the AST using `solidity-parser`.
2. Extract control flow nodes (e.g., statements, checks) and build the CFG.
3. Compute data dependencies to generate the DFG.
4. **Fusing Nodes**: We map nodes from AST, CFG, and DFG sharing the same source location into a single fused node. We add custom edge types (e.g., `ast_edge`, `control_flow_edge`, `data_flow_edge`) connecting them.

### Training & Batching

We utilized **PyTorch Geometric** and **Deep Graph Library (DGL)**. Because smart contracts can be very large, we utilized batch training with dynamic graph padding to fit multiple graphs into GPU memory.

```python
# Fusing distinct edge types into a heterogeneous graph
from torch_geometric.data import HeteroData

data = HeteroData()
# Add nodes
data['node'].x = node_features # Shape: [num_nodes, feature_dim]

# Add AST, CFG, and DFG edges
data['node', 'ast_link', 'node'].edge_index = ast_edges
data['node', 'control_link', 'node'].edge_index = cfg_edges
data['node', 'data_link', 'node'].edge_index = dfg_edges
```

### Key Findings

Our model was tested on datasets containing thousands of smart contracts (including reentrancy, integer overflow, and access control bugs). Fusing the three representations resulted in a **14% increase in recall** compared to models training on AST/CFG alone.

For more details on the implementation and research paper, visit [solidity-analyzer](https://github.com/SuZeAI/solidity-analyzer) and [GraphFusionVulDetect-Research](https://github.com/SuZeAI/GraphFusionVulDetect-Research).
